chunk_summary: Post-discharge nutrition for preterm infants with NEC or surgical bowel conditions focuses on addressing malabsorption, micronutrient deficiencies, and growth failure through specialized feeding strategies and close monitoring.
learning_objectives: Understand the nutritional challenges faced by preterm infants post-NEC or bowel surgery.
Learn strategies for managing malabsorption and micronutrient deficiencies in infants with ileal loss or enterostomy.
Identify appropriate feeding options, including human milk fortification and specialized formulas.
Recognize the importance of proactive monitoring and referral to gastroenterology when growth remains poor.
key_facts: Infants with ileal loss are at risk for vitamin B12 deficiency and zinc deficiency.
Human milk is the preferred base feed, but fortification or specialized formulas may be required for adequate nutrition.
Loose stools and poor growth may indicate carbohydrate malabsorption or formula intolerance, requiring hydrolyzed or elemental formulas.
Electrolytes should be closely monitored in cases of significant stool losses.
Structured nutrition escalation should precede gastroenterology referral if growth remains poor.
reference_quote: Infants with enterostomy or short bowel are at risk for zinc deficiency and other trace element losses; document whether additional supplementation is needed rather than relying on standard preterm vitamin drops alone. (University of Virginia)
document_key_topics: Management of NEC and surgical bowel conditions
Specialized feeding strategies and growth monitoring
